critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression disease severity prediction experiment has several fundamental methodological problems.

1. BIDIRECTIONAL LSTM ON CLINICAL TEMPORAL SEQUENCES USES FUTURE VISIT INFORMATION THAT IS NOT AVAILABLE AT PREDICTION TIME. The model is a bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM), which processes sequences in both forward and backward directions. The backward pass uses information from future visits relative to any given point in the sequence. In clinical deployment, predictions are made at the time of a visit -- future visits are not yet observed. A bidirectional LSTM trained on sequences that include future visits implicitly uses information that is causally unavailable at prediction time. This is not a data split leakage problem -- it is an architectural leakage problem that applies even within the training set.

2. ZERO-PADDING TO 24 VISITS CREATES A STRUCTURAL ARTIFACT FOR PATIENTS WITH FEW VISITS. The design pads all sequences to 24 visits, with patients having fewer visits zero-padded. The LSTM will encounter many zero-padded positions. If the proportion of padding differs systematically between high and low severity patients (e.g., high-severity patients have more visits and thus less padding), the LSTM will partially learn that high-padding patients are lower severity. This padding artifact is a structural signal leak from the sequence length to the target variable, and it is not controlled by the time-elapsed feature or the sequence index.

3. INTER-RATER RELIABILITY THRESHOLD OF ICC >= 0.7 IS APPLIED TO CLINICIAN SEVERITY ASSESSMENTS, BUT THE RESTRICTION TO HIGH-AGREEMENT CLINICIANS CHANGES THE TRAINING POPULATION. The design proposes restricting to high-agreement clinicians if ICC < 0.7. If only high-agreement clinicians are included, the resulting training and test populations are not the general patient population seen by all clinicians -- they are a selected subset corresponding to patients whose severity is easy to assess consistently. Models trained on this high-agreement subset may overestimate performance on the general population where clinician disagreement is higher and ground truth is noisier.

4. FORWARD-FILLING MISSING VALUES WITHIN A PATIENT TRAJECTORY ASSUMES STABILITY THAT MAY NOT HOLD. The design forward-fills missing symptom and lab values using the previous observation. Forward-fill implicitly assumes the value remains constant between observations. For lab values, this assumption is poor -- creatinine, CBC components, and inflammatory markers can change rapidly. A patient whose creatinine was 1.2 at visit n and deteriorated to 3.4 by visit n+2, with visit n+1 missing, will have a forward-filled creatinine of 1.2 at visit n+1 -- giving the model a false signal of stability just before a severe deterioration event. This is not a trivial artifact; it is a systematic misrepresentation of trajectory dynamics.

5. LOGISTIC REGRESSION BASELINE ON STATIC FEATURES ONLY IS A DELIBERATELY WEAK COMPARISON. The baseline uses only the most recent severity score plus static demographic and diagnosis features -- no temporal dynamics. Comparing a temporal model to an entirely atemporal baseline answers a different question than whether temporal modeling adds value: it also conflates the value of temporal history with the value of the particular architecture. A stronger baseline would be logistic regression on the full 24-visit history summarized by hand-engineered temporal features (mean severity, slope, max deviation), which would establish whether temporal modeling per se adds value over temporal feature engineering.
all_issues_raised: Bidirectional LSTM uses backward pass over future visits that are causally unavailable at clinical prediction time
Zero-padding to fixed sequence length creates a structural signal from sequence length to severity target
Restriction to high-ICC clinicians produces a selected training population that overestimates performance on the full clinical population
Forward-fill imputation misrepresents rapid changes in lab values, creating false stability signals before deterioration events
Logistic regression baseline on static features only does not isolate temporal modeling value from temporal feature engineering value
Logistic regression baseline uses only single most-recent visit; comparison confounds information access with model architecture
Bidirectional LSTM uses future visit information in the backward pass; inappropriate for prospective clinical prediction
Zero-padding creates spurious position-of-first-nonzero feature that distinguishes patients by history length; padding artifact not addressed
Forward-fill imputation ignores irregular visit intervals; staleness of imputed values not modeled
ICC < 0.7 exclusion of clinicians may select for severity extremes, creating selection bias in the remaining cohort
Bidirectional LSTM is architecturally inappropriate for clinical prediction because the backward pass introduces look-ahead leakage from future visits
Zero-padding for sparse patient sequences produces structured artifacts in LSTM representations that differ from legitimate missing data
Logistic regression baseline uses only the most recent severity score, which is weaker than an autoregressive trajectory baseline and understates what structured baselines can achieve
Restricting training to high-agreement clinicians if ICC < 0.7 creates a training distribution mismatch relative to the full test population
Forward-fill imputation underestimates severity for deteriorating patients by propagating last known values, biasing against the highest-risk subgroup
